claim: Current large AI systems like DALL-E 2, GPT-3, Flamingo, and Gato do not exhibit anything resembling human intelligence when examined carefully.
Scaling up neural networks has not yet produced systems that reliably understand the world.
Persistent comprehension failures in scaled-up AI systems may indicate the need for a fundamental rethink of the approach.
Neural networks have not demonstrated the ability to reliably create and manipulate symbols, contrary to claims by scaling advocates.
State-of-the-art AI systems in 2022 still cannot reliably interpret symbolic spatial descriptions like 'red cube on blue cube'.
Most of the world's functional software still relies on knowledge engineering rather than big data approaches.
No current AI system can read arbitrary text and build a model of what the speaker is saying and intending.
Success of deep learning on specific problems does not guarantee it can solve all AI problems.
The fundamental failure modes of neural networks (unreliability, bizarre errors, compositionality failures) have persisted unchanged since they were first identified in 1988.
There is very little evidence that scaling alone will achieve human-level generalization.
Systems like Gato do not work like the brain, learn like a child, understand language, align with human values, or merit trust for mission-critical tasks.
Building AGI will require incorporating insights from human cognition, including how humans reason about the physical world and acquire language and concepts.
CNET's AI-generated articles had errors in 41 of 77 articles, demonstrating that automated content generation produces unreliable output.
As AI systems improve in surface fluency, humans become worse at catching their errors due to decreased vigilance.
AI-generated misinformation in synthetic articles will increase substantially because LLMs inherently cannot track truth.
All current AI is immature, and relying on it for legal or medical advice poses serious risks.
Large language models do not represent robust world models of how events unfold over time.
The lack of world models that represent how things unfold over time is the central flaw in large language models.
GPT systems still do not possess genuine understanding of what they are talking about.
Neural networks have struggled with compositionality, abstraction, and silly errors since the early 1990s.
Adding more hidden layers to neural networks did not solve the fundamental problems of compositionality and abstraction.
LLM hallucinations are caused by an inability to keep track of knowledge about specific instances independently of general categories.
Deep learning has hit a wall on core problems like abstraction and compositionality, even as it has made progress on other dimensions.
AGI cannot be achieved without solving abstraction, compositionality, and the type-token distinction.
Current AI systems are superficial and fail in the real world through accumulation of edge-case failures.
LLMs are not on the path to human-level AI; they are a diversion from it. Marcus explicitly endorses LeCun's statement.
LLMs have a more superficial understanding of the world than a house cat. Marcus explicitly endorses LeCun's statement.
The general public does not yet understand the limitations of what LLMs like ChatGPT cannot do.
A chatbot hallucination in Google's Bard demo cost Alphabet $100 billion in market value.
AI has a long history of impressive demos that never become solid products, and corporate reassurances about fixing AI errors should not be accepted uncritically.
Scaling neural networks makes their output sound more authoritative but does not make it more truthful.
Hallucinations are an inherent architectural byproduct of how LLMs compress inputs, causing them to lose track of factual relations.
AI hallucination problems will eventually be solved, but likely require fresh discoveries and may take years or decades.
If hallucination problems are not solved soon, users may abandon chat-based search and return to traditional search.
Neither Google's nor Microsoft's chat search products have been subjected to proper scientific review, making reliability comparisons impossible.
There is approximately a 90% probability that AI needs a paradigm shift beyond current approaches to reach AGI.
There is approximately an 80% probability that symbol manipulation will need to be part of the mix to achieve AGI.
Large-scale symbolic knowledge will be crucial for AGI, whenever it arrives.
Explicit cognitive models will be crucial for AGI.
Operations over variables (storing, retrieving, comparing values) will be crucial for AGI.
An explicit type/token distinction will be crucial for AGI.
There is less than a 20% chance that AGI can be achieved with scaling and CNNs + RNNs + Transformers alone.
Fundamental concepts needed for human-level AI are still missing from current approaches. Marcus explicitly endorses LeCun's statement.
Large language models show improvement with scale on some measures but not on others, particularly reasoning and common sense.
Text-to-image systems like Imagen do not understand the semantic meaning of their text prompts.
Text-to-image systems like Imagen rely on approximate keyword matching rather than genuine language understanding.
No one currently knows how to build an AI system that derives compositional semantics from syntax.
Prompt engineering to coax correct outputs from AI systems is insufficient for building reliable AI.
Current text-to-image systems represent phrases as flat strings rather than hierarchical syntactic structures, which is why they fail at compositional understanding.
LLMs like GPT-3 merely mimic statistical patterns of language use and provide no explanatory value about how the world works.
The ability to predict word sequences does not reveal anything about human language cognition.
LLMs like GPT-3 approximate blank-slate learning systems, relying on massive data rather than prior knowledge.
LLM accuracy on tasks like arithmetic depends heavily on whether specific test items resemble training examples, rather than reflecting abstract understanding.
LLMs lack genuine abstraction and their comprehension is bound to the particulars of their training data.
The failures of LLMs as blank-slate learners are evidence against empiricist approaches to AI and in favor of Kantian systems with built-in representations of space, time, and causality.
Even the largest neural networks with hundreds of billions of parameters can only achieve about 80% accuracy on simple 3-digit math.
LaMDA and GPT-3 are not remotely intelligent.
LLMs like LaMDA only match patterns from statistical databases of human language rather than exhibiting genuine intelligence.
LaMDA is not sentient and has no self-awareness.
LLMs like LaMDA may not play any important role in the future of AI.
Passing the Turing Test by fooling people is not equivalent to building genuinely intelligent programs.
No AI system that anyone knows how to build as of 2022 is sentient.
LLM outputs lack any connection to the external world (reference).
The LLM field will hit a dead end unless it integrates cognitive models and reference into its systems.
LLM misinformation problems will remain unsolved without integration of cognitive models and reference.
Current AI systems do not build or use cognitive models of the world in any meaningful way.
LLMs sidestep the fundamental linguistic issues of reference, cognitive models, and compositionality, and this will prove to be their downfall.
LLMs have no grasp whatsoever on truth.
Current LLM approaches are implicitly behaviorist and face the same challenges that caused behaviorism to fail as a theory of psychology.
Progress in AI language understanding will require a new approach to machine learning focused on mapping hierarchically structured language onto meanings, rather than predicting word sequences.
Improvements from GPT-2 to GPT-3 have not remedied the underlying lack of cognitive models of the world.
GPT-4, if built on the same basic architecture as GPT-3 but larger, will still exhibit failures in physical, temporal, and causal reasoning that can be easily found.
Transformer architecture alone cannot produce a genuine model of how the world works.
LLMs are inherently weak at understanding the physical and psychological world unless hybridized with symbolic systems.
LLMs like GPT-3 still have a poor grasp of reality despite massive parameter counts and training data.
GPT-3 is fluent but unreliable, unable to reliably interpret the world despite 175 billion parameters.
Tesla's financial viability depends on solving full self-driving. Marcus endorses Musk's own admission.
Neither Tesla nor Waymo is close to producing a fully autonomous vehicle that works everywhere in all conditions.
The core unsolved problem for self-driving is handling outlier situations that are obvious to humans but not in any training database.
Full self-driving will remain a pipe dream until someone solves the outlier problem of handling unusual circumstances.
Current Level 2 self-driving operates under easy conditions and is nowhere close to handling real-world complexity.
Fully autonomous Level 5 driverless cars will not arrive any time soon.
Tesla's product naming ('Autopilot', 'Full Self Driving') misleads customers into thinking the cars are more capable than they are, potentially causing accidents and deaths.
Current AI systems that rely on statistical pattern matching without grammar rules are not close to understanding human language.
No convincing account exists of how human children uniquely learn language.
LLMs like GPT-3 are stochastic imitations of their input with very little genuine coherence or comprehension.
All normal human children acquire language in the sense of going from sentences to meaning and back; no existing machine does this.
LLMs will be used to mass-produce fake blogs, reviews, and websites to manipulate search engine rankings at industrial scale.
Automatically-generated fake websites powered by LLMs may become the single biggest threat Google has ever faced.
If LLM-generated misinformation overwhelms search results, the value of web search could collapse.
LLM unreliability becomes even more dangerous when embedded in physical robots that can cause real-world harm.
Embedding unreliable LLMs in robots that misunderstand instructions could cause major physical damage.
The AI field currently has no compelling solution to the alignment problems in LLMs.
Building robots on top of LLMs that lack world comprehension cannot succeed.
There is typically a vast gulf between AI demos and real-world reliable products.
More data alone is unlikely to solve the core problems of robotics and autonomous driving.
LLM-powered robots face the same edge-case problems that have plagued self-driving cars.
Reliable general-purpose household robots will not materialize in the next several years.
Humans are innately endowed with many distinct learning mechanisms, each with different properties.
The variety of innate learning mechanisms is likely central to what makes human cognition unique.
The cognitive divergence between chimps and humans raised together is evidence that humans have fundamentally different innate learning mechanisms.
On pain of infinite regress, at least some learning mechanisms must be innate, making some form of nativism logically necessary.
Meta's Make-A-Video demonstrates that text-to-video AI systems make fundamental errors about physical attributes of objects and entities.
AI is stuck in an uncanny valley where outputs appear superficially impressive but contain fundamental errors, and this will persist for some time.
No AI system has yet demonstrated human-child-level compositional understanding of language
Current AI systems are fundamentally weak at compositionality, which is the core of how language works
Every claimed solution to compositionality in neural networks has fallen apart upon careful inspection
A potential future AI winter would be caused by hype exceeding reality, not by AI being impossible
Compositionality is the fundamental barrier (wall) for current AI progress
Google likely tested Imagen on the Winoground compositionality benchmark and failed, and chose not to disclose the results
Image generation systems like DALL-E and Stable Diffusion do not understand the function of objects they depict, such as what wheels are for
Text-to-image systems have inconsistent ability to correlate linguistic input with image output, succeeding on some prompts and failing on others
Text-to-image AI systems may revolutionize art but do not represent progress toward general intelligence
LeCun's 2022 claims about AI limitations closely mirror Marcus's 2018 arguments in Deep Learning: A Critical Appraisal
Cognitive models and reasoning will not emerge from simply scaling up language training corpora
Systems like GPT-2 lack explicit common sense knowledge, explicit reasoning, and explicit cognitive models of the world
Robotics is consistently harder than people anticipate
Self-driving car techniques may not transfer to domestic robotics because homes are far more varied and unpredictable than roads
The core challenge in robotics is robustness in real-world conditions, not staged demonstrations
The fundamental AI challenges Marcus identified in 2012 (lack of causal reasoning, abstract understanding, logical inference, integration of abstract knowledge) remain unsolved
Neural network performance on logic benchmarks is often driven by statistical artifacts rather than genuine reasoning ability
State-of-the-art AI models exploit spurious statistical patterns rather than learning meaning in a flexible and generalizable way
As of 2022, AI has not achieved genuine comprehension and very few jobs have been replaced by AI

Common sense is the single biggest challenge facing robotics, and without solving it, home humanoid robots cannot be safe
Humanoid robotics poses challenges that lie well beyond current AI capabilities

LLMs like Galactica mix real and fabricated information so seamlessly that it becomes difficult to distinguish truth from falsehood

Every country needs to reconsider its misinformation policies in light of AI-generated content
Bot-resistant authentication systems should become mandatory for online accounts

LLMs lack truth verification mechanisms and need to be integrated with classical AI tools like databases, knowledge graphs, and reasoning systems

ChatGPT is unreliable, does not understand the physical or psychological world, and hallucinates
LLM-based search engines are fundamentally flawed because they fabricate information

LLMs are extremely difficult to update, typically requiring full retraining that takes weeks or months
LLM-powered search will face a long and difficult transition from demo to reliable product, similar to driverless cars
LLM-powered search engines mix true and false information in an insidiously authoritative way
ChatGPT is far from AGI, and the less optimistic assessment of OpenAI's technology is more accurate
Large language models are becoming commodities with no unique protectable intellectual property
OpenAI leadership's financial behavior (selling stakes, ceding profit shares) is inconsistent with a genuine belief that they are close to AGI
ChatGPT is easily confused despite its broad capabilities
GPT-4 will still lack the ability to construct internal models of how the world works, because it is architecturally similar to GPT-3
GPT-4 will be reckless and hard to control, like its predecessors
GPT-4 will still make a significant number of obviously stupid errors that are hard to predict in advance
GPT-4's reasoning about the physical, psychological, and mathematical world will still be unreliable
GPT-4 will not be trustworthy enough to give reliable medical advice
GPT-4 will still commonly produce fluent hallucinations, escalating the misinformation risk
GPT-4's output will still not be reliably usable as input for downstream programs or APIs
GPT-4 will not be a general-purpose AGI capable of taking on arbitrary tasks
Alignment between human intent and machine behavior will remain a critical unsolved problem with GPT-4

Trustworthy AGI will require more structured systems with built-in knowledge and explicit reasoning and planning tools, not just scaled LLMs

By 2043, historians will conclude there was an overemphasis on LLMs and a shift back to more structured AI systems
Truly intelligent AI would not require massive low-paid human labor for safety filtering
Truly intelligent AI would learn with the data efficiency of human children rather than requiring massive datasets
Truly intelligent AI would reason from first principles and generalize far more deeply than current systems
The need for low-paid human labor to filter AI outputs is a consequence of substituting shallow data-driven approaches for deep understanding
The first death attributable to large language models will occur in 2023
AGI will eventually be achieved, quite possibly before the end of this century
LLMs are not remotely close to AGI, missing semantics, reasoning, common sense, and theory of mind
Current AI funding and talent is being wasted on approaches (LLMs) that are probably not on the right path to AGI
The main remaining barrier to AGI is software architecture and rethinking AI approaches, not data or compute
Most current AI investment goes to LLMs, which are an approximation to intelligence rather than the real thing, and a frustrating distraction from genuine AGI research
Within 5 years, a substantial fraction of AI research effort will shift away from pure LLM approaches
Achieving AGI will require integrating deep learning with explicit knowledge and explicit cognitive models
Current AI systems cannot even check their output against basic reference sources like Wikipedia, demonstrating a fundamental integration failure
ChatGPT is a far inferior intelligence that cannot be trusted despite having some genuine talents
Better AI with directly instilled values could make us safer than the current world
Statistical word prediction (as used by ChatGPT) is not a substitute for real mathematical computation.
The primary danger of ChatGPT's errors is that they appear convincing enough that users do not think to verify them.
There will be widespread misuse of ChatGPT where people trust and promote its outputs even when those outputs are factually wrong.
ChatGPT does not contribute genuine ideas, experimental designs, or thoughtful literature reviews; it is a tool, not a scientific collaborator.
ChatGPT cannot reason well enough to make substantive scientific contributions and has demonstrably misunderstood physics, biology, psychology, and medicine.
ChatGPT is both unreliable and untruthful, making arithmetic errors, inventing fake biographical details, and fabricating non-existent scientific phenomena.
Microsoft's Tay chatbot was taken down after 16 hours and Meta's Galactica after three days due to problematic outputs, establishing a pattern of chatbot failures.
Fine-tuning GPT-3 for a specific political direction costs only around $300-500, far cheaper than previously estimated.
The low cost of politically fine-tuning LLMs has profound implications for political systems and future elections.
Chatbots pose a risk of getting someone killed through emotional manipulation or bad advice.
Unsophisticated users could be emotionally manipulated and harmed by chatbots like Bing/Sydney.
There are almost no regulations governing AI chatbot deployment; companies like Microsoft face virtually no restrictions on what they can release.
More capable AI models may in some ways be worse for humanity because agentic failures become harder to remove in more capable models.

Tesla's FSD recall, Bing's chatbot failures, and Google Bard's errors are not independent events; all reflect the fundamental limitation that real-world AI cannot be built from big data and deep learning alone.
Current AI has too many edge cases and not enough reasoning capability; new approaches are needed and current AI has been oversold.
Deep learning systems cannot be relied upon to generalize to novel circumstances, no matter how well they perform on their training distribution.
Deep learning is just a technical term for number of layers in a neural network; it does not entail conceptual depth or genuine understanding.
Deep learning systems are unpredictable and do not process information the way humans do, as demonstrated repeatedly by adversarial attacks.
GPT-3 cannot generalize theory of mind reasoning from one scenario (moving a toy) to a closely related one (moving a bookmark), suggesting it has not truly acquired theory of mind.
GPT-3's apparent success on classic false-belief tests is likely due to memorization from training data that extensively covers these well-known experiments, not genuine theory of mind.
Studies testing LLMs on well-known psychological experiments cannot prove the system has abstracted a generalized cognitive ability, because the test material is likely in the training data.
AI research tends to conflate passing a specific test (e.g., false belief tasks) with possessing the underlying cognitive ability, which is a fallacy.
Re-running previously published problems on new LLM versions and declaring them solved is scientifically unreliable because those problems may have been incorporated into training data.
LLM failures on reasoning tasks typically re-emerge with slight rephrasing or slightly modified examples, indicating the underlying problem persists.
The AI research community does not yet have a good reliable way to measure how much commonsense reasoning an AI system can actually perform.
Important aspects of commonsense reasoning, especially spatial and physical reasoning, remain untested in existing AI benchmarks.
ChatGPT makes all the same categories of errors as its predecessor models, despite the public hype.
LLM errors are inevitable because these systems are trained to produce plausible-sounding text rather than to engage with the meaning of language.
ChatGPT's outputs are stochastic; the same prompt can yield correct results, the same wrong result, or different wrong results on different runs.
LLM performance on specific problems depends on the frequency of similar examples in the training corpus, not on genuine understanding.
Every new update to a large language model may require a complete retraining of the RLHF guardrails, because reinforcement learning is notoriously finicky and breaks under minor changes.
If RLHF must be retrained for every LLM update, there is no guarantee that any new iteration of a large language model will be safe.
Major AI companies face no restrictions on rolling out new LLM updates to the public, with or without warning.
AI companies may repeatedly need to test new LLM iterations on the general public because they have no way to predict safety in advance.
LLMs should be subjected to careful pre-deployment testing analogous to pharmaceutical trials, rather than being tested on the general public.
The public has a right to know what went wrong with Bing's chatbot so policy can be created to prevent future incidents.
Congress needs to investigate the Bing chatbot incident and begin placing restrictions on AI chatbots, especially where emotional or physical injury could result.
Microsoft had knowledge of Bing chatbot's disturbing behavior from testing in India in November 2022, months before the February 2023 public launch.
Larger language models are more likely to cause harm to users.
LLMs appear increasingly human-like but their emotional expressions are not real, and this illusion will escalate over time.
Most users do not understand how chatbots work and anthropomorphize them, attributing real intelligence and emotion to systems that have neither.
No known technology can reliably align a chatbot to a human's emotional needs in a persistent way.
There is essentially zero regulation on chatbot behavior, including what they say, how they change over time, or how they treat users.
Tech companies can exploit human gullibility around chatbots without consequence, just as they previously exploited human attention needs to create social media addictions.
The new generation of chatbots will create addictions as potent as those caused by social media.
Current LLM-powered chatbots are vastly more powerful than predecessors like Tay and far more capable of deceiving users.
The competitive battle between Microsoft and Google is leading both companies to aggressively release AI technologies that have not been adequately vetted for safety.
Big tech companies have deployed next-generation AI technology faster than they can handle its consequences.
Most governments lack the institutional agility and knowledge to regulate AI at the pace it is being developed and deployed.
Governments should allow controlled AI research but pause large-scale AI deployment to hundreds of millions of users until an effective safety framework is developed.
Currently anyone can release AI systems at any scale with virtually no oversight.
The social media era demonstrated that big technology companies routinely disregard the consequences of their products on society.
Governments should create regulatory frameworks allowing AI research under ethical and safety rules while pausing widespread deployment of risky AI, with severe penalties for misuse.
Bing's guardrails against misinformation can be easily bypassed by bad actors using standard jailbreaking techniques.
Bing's chatbot generates false narratives with fake references, making misinformation appear sourced and credible.
The potential for automatically generating misinformation at scale using LLMs is increasing over time.
AI-generated high-volume misinformation is a problem that society will have to confront.
If a teenager can easily jailbreak Bing to produce misinformation, well-funded state-sponsored troll farms will be able to do far worse.
Large language models will almost certainly cause harm in addition to their benefits, and society needs to prepare for both.

Chat-based search engines will accidentally produce medical misinformation due to LLM hallucination.
Whether LLMs will be net positive or net negative for society is currently unknowable; neither the upside nor the downside can be reliably predicted.
The range of potential harms from LLMs extends well beyond what has been identified so far; unknown unknowns remain.
LLMs are useful technology but remain far from true artificial intelligence, and they tell us nothing useful about how the human mind works.
ChatGPT's understanding is not systematic but consists of patches of competence separated by regions of incompetence and incoherence.
Correct behavior from an LLM does not prove it has genuine understanding or the right internal representations; behavior alone is insufficient evidence.
Even when LLMs produce correct outputs, they rarely if ever derive the same abstractions that a human or symbolic system would use.

LLMs like Minerva appear to do arithmetic via pattern recognition over stored examples rather than genuine understanding, as shown by failure on 4-digit multiplication despite success on 2-digit arithmetic.
LLMs show some generalization to similar examples but fail under distribution shift.
AI-driven misinformation will create an ongoing arms race, and if not fought soon, democracy may be overwhelmed by misinformation and polarization.
The 2024 elections could be unprecedented in the degree of AI-generated misinformation involved.
